fix_plans.title: Raise adversarial loss weight to 2 and train 100 epochs
fix_plans.content: In train.py main(), modify the vae.train(...) call to (a) extend max_epochs to 100 by changing the argument from max_epochs=args.train_epochs to max_epochs=100 (or equivalently changing build_parser default for --train-epochs from 50 to 100), (b) extend the KL warmup window in plan_kwargs from n_epochs_kl_warmup=5 to n_epochs_kl_warmup=20 so the adversarial loss stays at full strength for longer before KL weight ramps up, and (c) replace scale_adversarial_loss='auto' with scale_adversarial_loss=2.0 to roughly double the gradient pressure the encoder receives for fooling the batch classifier. Keep adversarial_classifier=True, lr=1e-3, n_latent=30, n_hidden=256, dropout_rate=0.0, batch_size=128 unchanged. Rationale: round 4 lifted kBET 0.509->0.5455 with per-batch still 0.0/0.0 — the adversarial signal is working but is too weak to break the per-batch separation; scaling it up (a constant >1 weight, not 'auto' which decays with kl_weight) plus giving it more epochs to act should push per-batch acceptance above 0 and the mean above the 0.55 acceptable floor. The ELBO anchor will be slightly worse than round 4 (adversarial loss slows reconstruction) but the 100-epoch budget plus 20-epoch warmup keeps reconstruction healthy; the utility-floor guard is intact.

fix_plans.title: Increase batch-balance strength and subsample to 12000 cells
fix_plans.content: In train.py build_parser(), change two argparse defaults: --max-cells from 8000 to 12000 and --batch-balance-strength from 0.5 to 0.9. Leave everything else unchanged (train-epochs stays at 50, AdversarialTrainingPlan with scale_adversarial_loss='auto' stays, n_latent=30/n_hidden=256/dropout=0.0 stays, lr=1e-3 stays). Rationale: the kBET probe is computed on a stratified 2000-cell subsample with batch_codes drawn from the full dataset; per-batch acceptance has been stuck at exactly 0.0/0.0 across rounds 2-4, meaning every neighbourhood of batch-0 cells is dominated by batch-0 and vice versa. The current 8000-cell sample at balance_strength=0.5 keeps the natural ~51/49 batch imbalance, so the encoder has no incentive to mix at the neighbourhood level. Forcing a near-uniform batch prior in the training cell set (strength=0.9) and giving the model more cells (12000) means each minibatch is more balanced, the adversarial classifier sees both batches equally, and the latent is pushed toward a distribution where k-NN neighbourhoods are genuinely mixed. This is a data-handling lever — orthogonal to the optimization and architecture levers — so it stacks on top of the existing adversarial plan without touching plan_kwargs or model dims. ELBO anchor will improve slightly (more data) and the utility floor is safe.

fix_plans.title: Deepen encoder to two hidden layers and widen latent to 64
fix_plans.content: In train.py build_parser(), change three argparse defaults: --n-latent from 30 to 64, --n-hidden from 256 to 256 (unchanged value but consumed differently — see below), and add a new constantEncoderDepth by editing the scvi.model.SCVI(...) construction in main() to pass n_layers=2 (currently scvi.model.SCVI is constructed with n_latent, n_hidden, dropout_rate only; add n_layers=2 as a keyword argument). Leave train-epochs=50, adversarial plan, lr=1e-3, batch_size=128, max_cells=8000, batch_balance_strength=0.5 unchanged. Rationale: rounds 2-4 all used a single-hidden-layer encoder with n_latent<=30, and per-batch kBET has been pinned at 0.0/0.0 every round — the encoder is structurally collapsing each batch into its own region of latent space, and the adversarial classifier is trivially winning on a 10-30 dim latent. Widening to 64 dims and adding a second 256-hidden layer gives the encoder the capacity to represent a genuinely mixed manifold where per-batch neighbourhoods overlap, while the adversarial loss continues to push toward using that capacity for mixing rather than separation. This is an architecture lever (depth + width), distinct from the optimization lever (Plan 1) and the data lever (Plan 2). ELBO anchor may rise slightly with the bigger model (more parameters to fit) but will still decrease monotonically across epochs; the utility floor stays intact because the adversarial loss prevents capacity-driven overfitting to batch.
